title: Listening - Part 3: Taking Photographs
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a photograph they are particularly pleased with. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H why each speaker values their photograph. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a photograph they are particularly pleased with. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H why each speaker values their photograph. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about a photograph they took.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: My grandmother usually dislikes being photographed and immediately becomes formal whenever she notices a camera. One afternoon she was teaching my little cousin how to make bread, and I quietly took a picture while they were laughing at the flour all over the table. [19]I love the photo because her warm, playful character is obvious in her expression, rather than the serious face she puts on in posed pictures.[/19] She even asked for a copy, which is very unusual for her.||Speaker 2 Man: I enjoy photographing birds, though most of my pictures show a blur or an empty branch. Last spring I spent several mornings beside a lake waiting for a kingfisher. [20]Eventually I managed to catch the exact second it dived towards the water, which required waiting quietly, focusing quickly and getting the light just right before it disappeared again.[/20] It isn't perfect, but whenever I look at it, I remember how much patience and concentration it took to achieve.||Speaker 3 Woman: For an online photography challenge, we had to take a picture within ten minutes' walk of home. I complained that my neighbourhood was ordinary and there was nothing worth photographing. Then I went out shortly after sunrise. [21]I found light reflecting in puddles beside an old brick wall, and taking the picture made me see that even familiar streets can look striking when you pay proper attention.[/21] It didn't receive many votes online, but it changed the way I look at where I live.||Speaker 4 Man: My brother lives overseas and my sister works most weekends, so arranging a time when our whole family is together is almost impossible. For my parents' anniversary, everybody managed to return home for one afternoon. [22]I took a group photograph in the garden because I knew it might be years before my parents, their children and all the grandchildren were in the same place again.[/22] The picture isn't professionally composed, but it sits on my parents' wall and matters more than any technically better photograph.||Speaker 5 Woman: I was always nervous about heights, so when friends suggested walking across a high rope bridge during a holiday, I almost stayed behind. They didn't pressure me, and eventually I decided to try. Halfway across, I stopped to take a quick picture of the valley below. [23]Whenever I see that photograph, I remember that I managed something I had been convinced I couldn't do, rather than just remembering the beautiful view.[/23] I'm still not eager to climb towers, but I feel braver than before.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: My grandmother usually dislikes being photographed and immediately becomes formal whenever she notices a camera. One afternoon she was teaching my little cousin how to make bread, and I quietly took a picture while they were laughing at the flour all over the table. I love the photo because her warm, playful character is obvious in her expression, rather than the serious face she puts on in posed pictures. She even asked for a copy, which is very unusual for her.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I enjoy photographing birds, though most of my pictures show a blur or an empty branch. Last spring I spent several mornings beside a lake waiting for a kingfisher. Eventually I managed to catch the exact second it dived towards the water, which required waiting quietly, focusing quickly and getting the light just right before it disappeared again. It isn't perfect, but whenever I look at it, I remember how much patience and concentration it took to achieve.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: For an online photography challenge, we had to take a picture within ten minutes' walk of home. I complained that my neighbourhood was ordinary and there was nothing worth photographing. Then I went out shortly after sunrise. I found light reflecting in puddles beside an old brick wall, and taking the picture made me see that even familiar streets can look striking when you pay proper attention. It didn't receive many votes online, but it changed the way I look at where I live.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My brother lives overseas and my sister works most weekends, so arranging a time when our whole family is together is almost impossible. For my parents' anniversary, everybody managed to return home for one afternoon. I took a group photograph in the garden because I knew it might be years before my parents, their children and all the grandchildren were in the same place again. The picture isn't professionally composed, but it sits on my parents' wall and matters more than any technically better photograph.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I was always nervous about heights, so when friends suggested walking across a high rope bridge during a holiday, I almost stayed behind. They didn't pressure me, and eventually I decided to try. Halfway across, I stopped to take a quick picture of the valley below. Whenever I see that photograph, I remember that I managed something I had been convinced I couldn't do, rather than just remembering the beautiful view. I'm still not eager to climb towers, but I feel braver than before.

options.text: It records a rare family occasion.
It helped them notice local beauty.
It was technically difficult to take.
It reminds them of overcoming a fear.
It led to a new friendship.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The unplanned image captures the grandmother's natural, playful personality.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker is proud because catching a fast-moving bird in the correct light was challenging.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Taking the photograph revealed unexpected beauty in the speaker's everyday surroundings.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The photograph is valued because it shows the rarely assembled whole family.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The image is important because it recalls the speaker managing an activity despite a fear of heights.
